1: Standard prophetic introduction marking a fresh oracle. The phrase wayehi devar-YHWH elai ('the word of YHWH came to me') is Ezekiel's most frequent commissioning formula, occurring over 50 times in the book. Each occurrence marks the beginning of a discrete divine communication event, not merely an internal impression.
2: harim Yisrael ('mountains of Israel'): the mountains are addressed as if persons — a rhetorical device heightening the indictment. The high places on Israel's ridges were the actual sites of illicit worship; addressing the terrain itself implicates the geography of apostasy. Cf. 36:1 where the same mountains receive a word of consolation — structural reversal in the book.
3: bamoteichem ('your high places'): bamah (pl. bamot) designates open-air elevated shrines, condemned from Leviticus 17 through Kings. Here YHWH announces that the sword will destroy the very sites where the people worshipped false gods — judgment through the mechanism of defeat and military desecration of the sanctuaries. Nevatim ('demolished'): razed, used of altars.
4: chammanim ('incense altars'): etymology debated — possibly from chammah (sun), designating sun-pillars or incense stands associated with solar worship. The term appears in Leviticus 26:30 as a target of divine wrath. gillulim ('idols'): from gll (to roll), cognate with gelal (dung pellets) — a deliberately contemptuous coinage used 39x in Ezekiel alone. YHWH refuses to use a neutral word for the objects of Israel's devotion.
5: gufei bnei Yisrael ('corpses of the Israelites'): the ultimate irony — the bodies of worshippers laid before the idols they served. The verb natati ('I will put/give') makes YHWH the agent, not a passive observer. The dead scattered before their idols enacts a kind of dark liturgy: the worshippers finally brought fully into the presence of their gods.
6: yeshamu ('they shall be desolated/made waste'): Niphal of shamam, appearing 5x in this chapter. Shamam describes the condition of a land utterly stripped of population, agriculture, and life — the covenant curse of Leviticus 26:31-32 enacted. The high places that were crowded with worshippers will be as empty as the idols themselves.
7: yeda'tem ki-ani YHWH ('you will know that I am YHWH'): the refrain formula that punctuates all of Ezekiel — occurring ~72 times in the book. The goal of judgment is not annihilation but recognition: YHWH's actions are revelatory. The same formula is used for both judgment oracles (nations will know YHWH) and restoration oracles (Israel will know YHWH). Knowledge of YHWH is the telos of all divine action in Ezekiel.
8: palitim ('escapees/survivors'): from palat (to escape, deliver). The remnant that escapes the sword is not preserved through their own merit but scattered among the nations — a partial rescue that simultaneously continues the humiliation. YHWH preserves a witness to his judgment even in the dispersion.
9: lev zona ('whoring heart'): the marriage metaphor of apostasy that runs through Ezekiel 16, 20, and 23 is introduced here with its central term. Zana (to whore/commit harlotry) in prophetic usage describes covenantal infidelity — the exclusive relationship with YHWH violated by attraction to foreign gods. eineihem ha-zonot ('their eyes that whored after'): the eyes as the organ of desire that initiated the apostasy.
10: veyadeu ki-lo chinnam ('they shall know that not for nothing'): the survivors will finally understand that the destruction was not random or unjust — it was the enacted covenant curse for specific covenant violations. The recognition formula here adds the crucial element of justified judgment: YHWH acted with moral coherence, not capriciousness.
11: haka yad verego regel ('strike with the hand and stamp with the foot'): prophetic gestures of grief and horror — possibly also mimicking the executioner's final blow. The prophet embodies the divine anguish over the apostasy even as he announces judgment. Ezekiel's sign-acts throughout the book make his body the medium of prophetic communication.
12: The three modes of death announced — pestilence, sword, famine — are the classic biblical covenant-curse triad (Lev 26:25-26; Deut 28:21-22). The formula ve'yadeu ki-ani YHWH closes the triad, ensuring even the judgment itself serves the purpose of divine self-revelation.
13: raach nicho'ach ('pleasing/soothing aroma'): the technical term from Leviticus for the aroma of acceptable sacrifice that ascends to YHWH. Here the phrase is used bitterly — the pleasing aroma rising from the high places, under the trees, on the mountains, was worship given to other gods. Every element of true sacrifice (the aroma, the elevation, the open-air altar) is present but directed to the wrong recipient.
14: nosh'mamah umeshimmah ('desolate and waste'): the doubled desolation intensifies the completeness of the coming devastation. The phrase midbar diblata ('wilderness from Diblah/Riblah') marks the geographical extent of the destruction from south to north — the wilderness of Riblah marking the northern border of the land. The yeda'tem formula closes the chapter, making recognition of YHWH the final word.
